text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m locating the catalog page, the data-fetching path, and any existing cache or server-action patterns so I can wire the sync flow into the right layer without introducing extra blocking on reads.

text: I found a very small Next app, so I’m checking the current page and config first. If there’s no existing cache layer, I’ll add one around the product data fetch and keep the sync action scoped to invalidation plus background refresh semantics.

I’ve confirmed the relevant Next.js behavior: for read-heavy cached data, `unstable_cache` with tags is the right fit, and `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` gives the stale-while-revalidate behavior you want instead of forcing read requests to block. I’m updating the product data helper and the page together so the same tag drives both reads and invalidation.

I’m making the page itself the admin surface now: cached catalog read, inline server action for sync, and a simple browsing-first table so the behavior is visible end to end instead of only in the data layer.

The main wiring is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any type or Next.js cache-component issues, because this app is small enough that one compile pass will surface the remaining integration problems quickly.

text: Build surfaced a strict-TypeScript issue only, not a cache or server-action problem. I’m tightening the helper types and then rerunning the build; the cache behavior itself compiled cleanly.

I only need one follow-up change: the page is still getting `any` from the JavaScript data helper, which strict TypeScript flags. I’m adding a local product type and an explicit cast so the build stays clean without changing the runtime behavior.

text:
Implemented the catalog sync flow with cached reads and background invalidation.

- [`app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx#L1) now renders an admin catalog page, uses a regular HTML `<form>`, and wires the inline Server Action to `revalidateTag("products", "max")` so admins can submit sync and keep working immediately.
- [`lib/db.js`](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js#L1) now caches catalog reads with `"use cache"` and `cacheTag("products")`, so the same catalog data is reused across requests until the tag is invalidated.
- [`next.config.ts`](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts#L1) enables `cacheComponents`, which is required for the cache directives.
- [`tsconfig.json`](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json#L1) was updated by Next.js during the build to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`.

Verification:
- `npm run build` passes.

Docs used:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache
